moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Scandinavian Defense (also called the Center Counter), one of the oldest defenses to 1.e4. White plays 1.e4, claiming central space. The Scandinavian is direct and practical — Black immediately challenges e4 with ...d5, leading to positions where Black trades central presence for rapid piece development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn in the center, controlling d5 and f5.
plans: Build a strong center and develop pieces quickly
Open lines for the queen and bishop

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...d5, the Scandinavian Defense — striking at e4 immediately without any preparation. This is the most direct possible challenge to White's center, forcing an exchange on move two. The downside is that after exd5, Black must recapture with the queen (developing it early) or a pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After exd5 Qxd5, Black will have no center pawns while White has a free hand to build one.
plans: Recapture with the queen and develop quickly despite the early queen sortie
Place pieces actively and aim for a solid, harmonious position
alternatives: 1...c5 (Sicilian) — less committal, creates asymmetry without moving the quee...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures exd5, which is virtually forced. Declining with e5 or d3 would give Black an equal or superior center. After exd5, Black faces the defining decision of the Scandinavian: recapture with the queen (main line, active but the queen gets harassed) or ...Nf6 (Marshall Gambit, sacrificing a pawn for development).
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White has a pawn on d5. If Black takes with the queen, White will get a free tempo with Nc3 develo.
plans: Develop Nc3 with tempo against Black's queen
Build a strong center with d4
alternatives: 2.Nc3 — declining the exchange, rare but playable

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures Qxd5, entering the main line Scandinavian. The queen is out early and will be harassed by Nc3, but Black's compensation is clear: the d-file is open, all pieces can develop freely, and Black's pawn structure is completely healthy with no weaknesses.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has no center pawns yet, but the position is completely open.
plans: Retreat the queen to a5 after Nc3, maintaining diagonal pressure
Develop minor pieces quickly: ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6
alternatives: 2...Nf6 (Modern Scandinavian) — gambits the pawn temporarily, avoids the earl...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops Nc3 with tempo, attacking the queen and developing a piece simultaneously. The Nc3 is the ideal response — it gains time, controls d5 and e4, and forces the queen to declare its position.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change. White prepares d4 for a strong center.
plans: Follow up with d4 to build a powerful center
Develop rapidly while Black's queen is wandering

moveAnnotations.annotation: The queen retreats to a5, the most popular and arguably best square. From a5, the queen pins the Nc3 along the a5-e1 diagonal (the knight can't move without exposing the king), keeps an eye on the e1 square for future tactics, and stays active on the a5-d8 diagonal.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black will build a center with ...c6 and ...e6.
plans: Develop ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6 for a solid setup
Use the queen's position on a5 to create pin-based threats
alternatives: 3...Qd6 — blocks the d-pawn and the dark-squared bishop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White builds the center with d4, establishing a strong d4 pawn that controls c5 and e5. Combined with the Nc3 supporting e4, White has a classical pawn center. The Scandinavian's challenge is that White gets this center easily — Black must find counterplay through piece activity.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White has a strong d4 pawn supported by the c3 knight.
plans: Complete development with Nf3, Bc4 or Bd3
Use the space advantage for a middlegame initiative

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the ideal square controlling d5, e4, and preparing kingside castling. The Nf6 pressures White's center and develops the most important minor piece — in the Scandinavian, rapid piece development is Black's primary compensation for the early queen moves.
plans: Continue with ...Bf5 to develop the light-squared bishop actively
Prepare ...e6 and ...Bb4 or ...Be7 for a solid setup

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, defending d4, controlling e5, and preparing to castle. The Nf3 completes White's kingside minor piece development and adds another defender to the center.
plans: Develop the bishops and castle quickly
Prepare Bc4 or Bd3 targeting Black's kingside

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays Bf5, developing the light-squared bishop to its most active square — just like in the Caro-Kann. The Bf5 gets the bishop outside the pawn chain BEFORE playing ...e6, which is the strategic key. From f5, the bishop controls the b1-h7 diagonal and pressures c2.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black hasn't committed to a pawn structure yet.
plans: Play ...e6 next to solidify, knowing the bishop is already active
Maintain the bishop on f5 as Black's best piece
alternatives: 5...Bg4 — pins the knight but can be challenged by Be2 and h3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to c4, targeting f7 — the weakest square near Black's king. The Bc4 is the most aggressive placement, supporting tactical ideas and creating pressure that Black must address. The a2-g8 diagonal is a constant source of threats.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop on c4 creates tactical tension around f7 and d5.
plans: Create threats against f7 and prepare Nd5
Castle and build a strong attacking position
alternatives: 6.Bd3 — targets the f5 bishop directly, aiming for a trade

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6, solidifying the center and protecting both d5 and f7. This was always the plan — develop the Bf5 first, THEN play ...e6. The c6+e6 pawn structure creates a compact fortress that is very difficult for White to crack.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The e6 pawn creates a solid chain with c6 (coming next).
plans: Play ...c6 to complete the solid pawn structure
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to d2, a flexible square that connects the rooks and prepares queenside castling (O-O-O). The Bd2 also supports Nc3, which was pinned by the Qa5, and keeps options open for both queenside and kingside castling.
plans: Consider queenside castling for aggressive play
Keep pieces coordinated and prepare to seize the initiative
alternatives: 7.O-O — simple kingside castling, very solid

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the trademark Scandinavian pawn structure with c6 — the c6+e6 compact fortress. The c6 pawn supports a future ...d5 if needed, controls b5 and d5, and creates a solid wall that resists White's central majority. This structure is the backbone of Black's Scandinavian setup.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c6-e6 structure is Black's bread and butter in the Scandinavian.
plans: Develop ...Bb4 or ...Be7 and castle kingside
Look for ...Nd5 or ...c5 breaks in the middlegame

moveAnnotations.annotation: White leaps to Nd5, occupying the ideal central outpost. The Nd5 attacks the Qa5 and the e7 pawn simultaneously, forcing the queen to retreat. From d5, the knight controls c7, e7, b6, f6, and c3 — it's the most powerful piece on the board.
plans: Force Black to deal with the strong knight
If ...Nxd5, recapture and maintain central dominance

moveAnnotations.annotation: The queen retreats to d8, the only safe square. It looks passive, but Black's position is actually solid — the compact c6+e6 structure holds firm, and the queen can redeploy to c7 or b6 later. The key is that Black's pieces are all well-placed despite the retreat.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The key battle is still around the d5 knight.
plans: Prepare to challenge or trade the d5 knight
Complete development and castle
alternatives: 9...Qb6 — keeps the queen active but exposed to potential Nb4-c2 ideas

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures Nxf6+ with check, forcing Black to recapture. This exchange removes the Nd5 but also eliminates Black's Nf6 — a key defensive piece. White trades the dominant knight for structural damage to Black's kingside.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After ...gxf6, Black's kingside pawns are shattered but the bishop pair and open g-file provide co.
plans: Exploit Black's structural weaknesses after the recapture
Use the open lines created by the exchange

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures gxf6, the more aggressive choice that keeps the center solid. Yes, the kingside pawns are damaged (doubled f-pawns and an open g-file), but Black gets the half-open g-file for the rook and retains the bishop pair. The Bf5 and the open g-file provide real attacking chances.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has doubled f-pawns and an exposed king, but the open g-file and bishop pair offer dynamic c.
plans: Use the open g-file for rook activity
Leverage the bishop pair in an open position
alternatives: 9...exf6 — keeps the structure intact but Black's center is less dynamic and ...

moveAnnotations.annotation: The king's pawn advances two squares, immediately seizing central space and opening lines for the bishop and queen.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a central pawn presence, controlling key dark squares in the center and preparing to.
plans: Develop pieces rapidly toward the center
Castle kingside for safety
alternatives: 1.d4 leads to queen's pawn openings with different central control

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Scandinavian Defence! Black immediately challenges White's central pawn, refusing to allow White to build a strong pawn center unopposed.
plans: Recapture on d5 with the queen
Develop pieces actively despite early queen movement
alternatives: 1...e5 leads to the King's Pawn Game

moveAnnotations.annotation: White accepts the pawn sacrifice, capturing toward the center and opening the e-file for the potential rook.
plans: Develop knights toward the center
Prepare to harass Black's early queen
alternatives: Declining with 2.e5 is rare but leads to advance variations

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures with the queen, restoring material equality but violating the opening principle of early queen development.
plans: Move the queen to a safer square when attacked
Develop minor pieces to support the position
alternatives: 2...Nf6 (Modern Scandinavian) delays queen recapture

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen's knight with tempo, attacking the Black queen and forcing it to move. This active move also controls important central squares like d5 and e4.
plans: Continue developing pieces
Castle kingside after more development
alternatives: 3.Nf3 develops without giving check but is less forcing

moveAnnotations.annotation: The queen retreats to a5, finding an active square that avoids further attacks while maintaining pressure on White's position. From a5, the queen eyes the knight on c3 and controls the a5-d8 diagonal.
plans: Develop minor pieces harmoniously
Consider castling queenside if White castles kingside
alternatives: 3...Qd6 or 3...Qd8 are more passive retreats

moveAnnotations.annotation: White establishes a strong pawn center with the classical two-pawn formation. This move controls key central squares, opens lines for the light-squared bishop, and prepares to support piece developm.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has the ideal pawn center with pawns on d4 and e4, controlling c5, d5, e5, and f5 square.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop
Castle kingside
alternatives: Other moves like 4.Nf3 develop pieces first

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight to its ideal square, attacking the central e4 square and preparing kingside development.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop
Prepare castling kingside
alternatives: 4...c6 immediately supports the center but is less active

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the kingside knight, defending the e4 pawn and controlling key central squares. This natural development move prepares kingside castling while maintaining central pressure.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop
Castle kingside for safety
alternatives: 5.f3 supports e4 but blocks the knight's natural square

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the light-squared bishop to an active square outside the pawn chain.
plans: Play ...e6 to complete development
Consider ...Nbd7 to support the center
alternatives: 5...Bg4 pins the knight but allows h3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the light-squared bishop to its most aggressive square, targeting the f7 pawn and controlling the long diagonal.
plans: Castle kingside
Look for tactical opportunities on the kingside
alternatives: 6.Be2 is more solid but less aggressive

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies the pawn structure with this critical move, supporting the center and preparing to complete kingside development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn structure with pawns on c7, e6, and f7, controlling key central squares.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop
Complete castling preparation
alternatives: 6...c6 supports the center but delays development

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the dark-squared bishop, connecting the rooks and preparing potential queenside castling. This move also supports the knight on c3 and prepares to meet Black's ...Bb4 pin.
plans: Consider queenside castling
Maintain central pressure
alternatives: 7.0-0 castles immediately but allows ...Bb4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black supports the center with this solid pawn move, controlling the d5 and b5 squares while preparing potential queenside expansion.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's pawn structure becomes more solid with pawns on c6 and e6, creating a strong foundation wh.
plans: Develop the queenside knight
Consider ...Be7 to complete development
alternatives: 7...Nbd7 develops immediately but doesn't support the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen to a central square, connecting the rooks and preparing potential kingside or queenside castling.
plans: Decide on castling direction
Maintain central control
alternatives: 8.0-0 castles immediately

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the dark-squared bishop with the strong pin against the knight on c3. This active move puts pressure on White's position while completing Black's piece development.
plans: Consider castling kingside
Maintain the pin pressure
alternatives: 8...Be7 is more solid but less active

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles queenside, placing the king on the safer queenside while bringing the rook into active play.
plans: Launch a kingside pawn storm
Use the h1 rook for kingside attack
alternatives: 9.0-0 castles kingside but is less aggressive

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the development by bringing the queenside knight into play, supporting the center and preparing to resolve the pin on the bishop.
plans: Decide on castling direction
Consider ...Nb6 to challenge the bishop
alternatives: 9...Na6 keeps the knight closer to the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Nf6 Variation (Modern) is an important variation of the Scandinavian Defence. Instead of recapturing with the queen, Black plays ...Nf6 and recaptures with the knight.
Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Nf6 Variation (Modern).
exd5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nxd5 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
d5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
Na5. This is the typical position arising from the Nf6 Variation (Modern). From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black moves the knight to f6. The knight on f6 improves Black's piece coordination and flexibility.
In the Scandinavian Defence, the c4 Tempo Gain is a dangerous line you must be aware of. In the ...Nf6 variation, White's c4 push gains time against the knight and establishes a strong center.
Nxd5 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
White opens with the king's pawn. Against the Scandinavian, this pawn will be challenged immediately on the next move.
The Scandinavian Defence! Black challenges e4 immediately, the most direct response possible. The downside is that Black's queen will be exposed after the exchange.
White captures, practically forced. Now Black must decide how to recapture — the queen recapture is the main line of the Scandinavian.
Black takes back with the queen. The queen is exposed in the center, but Black has eliminated White's e4 pawn and will develop quickly.
White develops with tempo, attacking the queen and gaining time. The knight on c3 controls d5 and e4.
The Qa5 variation! The queen retreats to a5 where it stays active on the queenside, pins the c3 knight along the a5-e1 diagonal, and avoids being pushed around further.
White seizes the center with d4, establishing the ideal pawn duo. White plans rapid development and will try to exploit Black's early queen move.
Black develops the knight to its best square, attacking e4 (now empty) and controlling d5. Solid, natural development.
White develops the kingside knight, supporting d4 and preparing to castle. Classical development.
Black develops the bishop outside the pawn chain before playing ...e6. This is a key advantage of the Scandinavian — the light-squared bishop is active, unlike in the French Defence.
White places the bishop on an aggressive diagonal, targeting f7 and controlling d5. The bishop is well-placed for kingside attacking chances.
Black solidifies the center and prepares to develop the dark-squared bishop. The pawn on e6 supports d5 if Black needs to place a pawn there later.
White develops the bishop, connecting the queen to the a5-square and preparing to potentially harass Black's queen. The bishop also prepares queenside castling in some lines.
Black reinforces control of d5 and prepares ...Bb4 to pin the knight. The c6 pawn creates a solid central structure and gives the queen escape squares.
White's queen moves to e2, supporting the e-file and preparing queenside castling. The queen on e2 can also support an e4-e5 push in some lines and keeps flexible options.
Black pins the c3 knight to the king, increasing pressure on White's center. This is a critical move in the ...Qa5 Scandinavian — the pin on c3 restricts White's options.
White castles queenside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. With opposite-side castling possible, the position can become very sharp.
Black develops the last minor piece, preparing to castle kingside and completing development. The knight on d7 supports key squares and can reroute to b6 or e5.
